title: Fauconshawe - A Ballad
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To fetch clear water out of the spring
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The little maid Margaret ran;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From the stream to the castleן¿½s western wing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It was but a bowshot span;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the sedgy brink where the osiers cling
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lay a dead man, pallid and wan.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The lady Mabel rose from her bed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And walked in the castle hall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where the porch through the western turret led
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She met with her handmaid small.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½What aileth thee, Margaret?ן¿½ the lady said,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Hast let thy pitcher fall?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Say, what hast thou seen by the streamlet side,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A nymph or a water sprite,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That thou comest with eyes so wild and wide,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And with cheeks so ghostly white?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Nor nymph nor sprite,ן¿½ the maiden cried,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But the corpse of a slaughtered knight.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The lady Mabel summonן¿½d straight
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To her presence Sir Hugh de Vere,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the guests who tarried within the gate
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of Fauconshawe most dear
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was he to that lady; betrothed in state
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They had been since many a year.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Little Margaret sayeth a dead man lies
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By the western spring, Sir Hugh;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I can scarce believe that the maiden lies,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet scarce can believe her true.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the knight replies, ן¿½Till we test her eyes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let her words gain credence due.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Down the rocky path knight and lady led,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While guests and retainers bold
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Followed in haste, for like wildfire spread
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The news by the maiden told.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They found ן¿½twas even as she had said,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The corpse had some while been cold.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How the spirit had passן¿½d in the moments last
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was little trace to reveal:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the still calm face lay no imprint ghast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Save the angelן¿½s solemn seal,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet the hands were clenchן¿½d in a death-grip fast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the sods stampן¿½d down by the heel.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sir Hugh by the side of the dead man knelt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Said, ן¿½Full well these features I know,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We have faced each other where blows were dealt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he was a stalwart foe;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I had rather have met him hilt to hilt
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than have found him lying low.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He turnן¿½d the body up on its face,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And never a word was spoken,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While he rippן¿½d the doublet, and tore the lace,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And tuggן¿½d, by the self-same token,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And strainן¿½d, till he wrenchן¿½d it out of its place,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The dagger-blade that was broken.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then he turned the body over again,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And said, while he rose upright,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½May the brand of Cain, with its withering stain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the murdererן¿½s forehead light,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For he never was slain on the open plain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor yet in the open fight.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Solemn and stern were the words he spoke,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he lookן¿½d at his ladyן¿½s men,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But his speech no answering echoes woke,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All were silent there and then,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till a clear, cold voice the silence broke:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lady Mabel cried, ן¿½Amen.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His glance met hers, the twain stood hushן¿½d,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With the dead between them there;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the blood to her snowy temples rushן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till it tinged the roots of her hair,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then paled, but a thin red streak still flushן¿½d
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the midst of her forehead fair.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Four yeomen raised the corpse from the ground,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At a sign from Sir Hugh de Vere;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It was borne to the western turret round,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And laid on a knightly bier,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With never a sob nor a mourning sound,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No friend to the dead was near.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet that night was neither revel nor dance
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the halls of Fauconshawe;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Men looked askance with a doubtful glance
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At Sir Hugh, for they stood in awe
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of his prowess, but he, like one in a trance,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Regarded naught that he saw.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Night black and chill, wind gathering still,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With its wail in the turret tall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And its headlong blast like a catapult cast
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the crest of the outer wall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And its hail and rain on the crashing pane,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till the glassy splinters fall.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A moody knight by the fitful light
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the great hall fire below;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A corpse upstairs, and a woman at prayers,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Will they profit her, aye or no?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Byן¿½r lady fain, anן¿½ she comfort gain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is comfort for us also.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The guests were gone, save Sir Hugh alone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he watched the gleams that broke
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the pale hearth-stone, and flickered and shone
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the panels of polishן¿½d oak;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He was ן¿½ware of no presence except his own
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till the voice of young Margaret spoke:


ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½ve risen, Sir Hugh, at the mirk midnight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I cannot sleep in my bed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now, unless my tale can be told aright,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I wot it were best unsaid;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It lies, the blood of yon northern knight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On my ladyן¿½s hand and head.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Oh! the wild wind raves and rushes along,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But thy ravings seem more wild,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She never could do so foul a wrong,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet I blame thee not, my child,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the feverן¿½d dreams on thy rest that throng!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He frownן¿½d though his speech was mild.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Let storm winds eddy, and scream, and hurl
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their wrath, they disturb me naught;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The daughter she of a high-born earl,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No secret of hers Iן¿½ve sought;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I am but the child of a peasant churl,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet look to the proofs Iן¿½ve brought;

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½This dagger snappן¿½d so close to the hilt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dost remember thy token well?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Will it match with the broken blade that spilt
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His life in the western dell?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nay! read her handwriting anן¿½ thou wilt,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From her paramourן¿½s breast it fell.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The knight in silence the letter read,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! the characters well he knew!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And his face might have matchן¿½d the face of the dead,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So ashen white was its hue!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then he tore the parchment shred by shred,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the strips in the flames he threw.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he muttered, ן¿½Densely those shadows fall
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the copse where the alders thicken;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There she bade him come to her, once for all,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now, I well may shudder and sicken;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gramercy! that hand so white and small,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How strongly it must have stricken.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ -

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At midnight hour, in the western tower,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Alone with the dead man there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lady Mabel kneels, nor heeds nor feels
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The shock of the rushing air,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though the gusts that pass through the riven glass
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Have scattered her raven hair.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Across the floor, through the opening door,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where standeth a stately knight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The lamplight streams, and flickers, and gleams,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On his features stern and white,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tis Sir Hugh de Vere, and he cometh more near,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the lady standeth upright.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Tis little,ן¿½ he said, ן¿½that I know or care
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the guilt (if guilt there be)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That lies ן¿½twixt thee and yon dead man there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nor matters it now to me;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I thought thee pure, thou art only fair,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And to-morrow I cross the sea.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He perishן¿½d! I ask not why or how?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I come to recall my troth;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Take back, my lady, thy broken vow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Give back my allegiance oath;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let the past be buried between us now
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For ever, ן¿½tis best for both.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Yet, Mabel, I could ask, dost thou dare
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lay hand on that corpseן¿½s heart,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And call on thy Maker, and boldly swear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That thou hadst in his death no part?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I ask not, while threescore proofs I share
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With one doubt, uncondemnן¿½d thou art.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! cold and bleak upon Mabelן¿½s cheek
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Came the blast of the storm-wind keen,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And her tresses black, as the glossy back
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the raven, glanced between
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her fingers slight, like the ivory white,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As she parted their sable sheen.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet with steady lip, and with fearless eye,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And with cheek like the flush of dawn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Unflinchingly she spoke in reply,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Go hence with the break of morn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I will neither confess, nor yet deny,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I will return thee scorn for scorn.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The knight bowן¿½d low as he turnן¿½d to go;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He travellן¿½d by land and sea,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But naught of his future fate I know,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And naught of his fair ladye;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My story is told as, long ago,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ My story was told to me.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Adam Lindsay Gordon represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Adam Lindsay Gordon's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
